 Pearson: helping people progress, everywhere
 Pearson aspires to be the world's leading learning company. Our aim is to help everyone progress in

 they are in the world. We've been involved in education for over 150 years, and by working across 70

 General Marking Guidance
    • All candidates must receive the same treatment. Examiners must mark the first candidate in
    exactly the same way as they mark the last.
    • Mark schemes should be applied positively. Candidates must be rewarded for what they have
    shown they can do rather than penalised for omissions.
    • Examiners should mark according to the mark scheme not according to their perception of where
    the grade boundaries may lie.
    • There is no ceiling on achievement. All marks on the mark scheme should be used appropriately.
    • All the marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Examiners should always award
    full marks if deserved, i.e. if the answer matches the mark scheme. Examiners should also be
    prepared to award zero marks if the candidate's response is not worthy of credit according to the
    mark scheme.
    • Where some judgement is required, mark schemes will provide the principles by which marks will
    be awarded and exemplification may be limited.
    • When examiners are in doubt regarding the application of the mark scheme to a candidate's
    response, the team leader must be consulted.
    • Crossed out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an alternative
    response.


9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 3

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
 Section A · Question 1
 Case-study context: the global semiconductor industry, the CHIPS Act, and the US–China technology
 decoupling.

      Question       With reference to Extract A and using a suitable market diagram, analyse                           Mark
      Number         why the global market for leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing is
                     dominated by such a small number of firms.

                     Indicative content

      1(a)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 2, Evaluation 2                              (8)

                          • Diagram (required): LRAC falling throughout the relevant output range
                          showing that a small number of firms can achieve minimum efficient scale
                          (natural oligopoly); or a high-barriers-to-entry oligopoly diagram. Axes and
                          curves must be correctly labelled.
                          • Extract A · a single leading-edge fab costs US$15–30 billion and takes
                          3–5 years to bring on-line. TSMC alone accounts for 60% of foundry output
                          and 90%+ at ≤5nm.
                          • Reasons for concentration: (i) extreme sunk fixed cost · fabs cannot be
                          easily repurposed; (ii) minimum efficient scale is very large relative to
                          demand · LRAC falls over most of the relevant range; (iii) learning-curve
                          effects · yield on advanced nodes takes years to mature; (iv)
                          agglomeration economies in Hsinchu/elsewhere · specialised suppliers,
                          labour pool, tacit knowledge; (v) patents and IP entrench incumbents; (vi)
                          capital-market access · only a few firms can raise the required capital.
                          • Upstream dependencies reinforce concentration: ASML is the sole global
                          producer of EUV lithography; EDA software is a three-firm oligopoly.
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) pace of technological change · new
                          architectures (chiplets, advanced packaging) may create entry windows; (ii)
                          government subsidy · CHIPS Act and China's Big Fund artificially lower
                          effective entry cost; (iii) node maturity · older (28nm+) nodes are far less
                          concentrated.
                          • Judgement: structural forces strongly favour continued concentration at
                          the leading edge, though policy may slow the trend.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–5          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are developed. Judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      6–8          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen. Arguments are developed, using logical, coherent
                               chains of reasoning. A balanced awareness of competing arguments.

    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.
 Where questions do not specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must select relevant examples from their own knowledge, these must be directly connected to
    the context/issues set out in the question, and

9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 5

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      Question       With reference to Extracts B and C and Figures 1 and 2, discuss the                                Mark
      Number         macroeconomic consequences for the United States of the concentration of
                     semiconductor production in East Asia.

                     Indicative content

      1(b)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 3, Evaluation 3                              (10)

                          • Knowledge: macro indicators · GDP, inflation, unemployment, current
                          account, national security externality. Resilience-vs-efficiency trade-off.
                          • Figure 1 · US share of global production fell from 37% (1990) to 11%
                          (2024); Figure 2 · US announced $223bn capex. Extract C · Taiwan
                          concentration; Rhodium-estimated multi-trillion-dollar global GDP hit from
                          disruption.
                          • Negative consequences: (i) supply-chain vulnerability · Taiwan
                          contingency could cut US GDP by 3–5% in severe scenarios; (ii)
                          national-security exposure · defence systems depend on leading-edge
                          chips; (iii) ICT trade deficit; (iv) reduced dynamic comparative advantage ·
                          loss of tacit manufacturing knowledge; (v) single-point-of-failure inflation
                          vulnerability.
                          • Positive/mitigating: (i) US retains dominance in highest-value segments ·
                          design (Nvidia, Qualcomm), EDA, equipment (Applied Materials, Lam), IP;
                          US share of value captured is far larger than share of production; (ii)
                          offshoring has historically produced lower consumer prices; (iii) CHIPS Act
                          capex ($223bn) is already reshoring some capacity.
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) probability of actual disruption · low but
                          non-negligible; (ii) time horizon · short-run exposure high, long-run
                          depends on reshoring success; (iii) counterfactual cost of self-sufficiency ·
                          significant fiscal cost and likely higher consumer prices; (iv) whether
                          'friendshoring' to allies (Japan, Korea, Netherlands) is a sufficient substitute.
                          • Judgement: concentration is a material macro risk that justifies some but
                          not complete reshoring, given the significant cost of full self-sufficiency.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–4          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented but with limited attempt to address
                               the question. Comparisons and judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      5–7          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, clear chains of reasoning, with well-developed arguments. An
                               awareness of the significance of competing arguments is present although this may
                               lack balance.

     Level 4      8–10         Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen, logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full
                               understanding of the question. Arguments are developed and evaluated. A full and
                               balanced awareness of the validity and significance of competing arguments.

 Demonstrating application (AO2) in responses

    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus
    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.

9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 7

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      Question       Discuss the extent to which the US CHIPS Act and the EU Chips Act can be                           Mark
      Number         justified on grounds of economic efficiency, with reference to Extracts A and
                     B.

                     Indicative content

      1(c)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 4, Evaluation 4                              (12)

                          • Knowledge: productive, allocative, dynamic efficiency; positive production
                          externalities (resilience, knowledge spillovers); infant-industry argument;
                          public-good aspects of national defence; government failure · rent
                          capture, subsidy race, distortion of comparative advantage.
                          • Extract B · US CHIPS Act $52bn + $24bn tax credits triggering $450bn+
                          private commitments; EU Chips Act €43bn; China Big Fund III $47.5bn.
                          Figure 2 · announced capex: US $223bn, China $142bn, Taiwan $148bn.
                          • Efficiency arguments FOR: (i) positive production externality · resilience
                          and national security are public goods and under-supplied by the market; (ii)
                          knowledge spillovers · semiconductor R&D; has large horizontal
                          spillovers; (iii) coordination failures · infrastructure, talent pipelines,
                          specialised suppliers require coordinated investment; (iv) infant-industry
                          case for revived US capacity; (v) other countries subsidise · level playing
                          field argument.
                          • Efficiency arguments AGAINST: (i) departure from comparative advantage
                          · TSMC Arizona fab is ~30% more expensive per wafer than Taiwan; (ii)
                          subsidy race · the four major jurisdictions together subsidise $160bn+; (iii)
                          rent capture · public funds flow to profitable firms' shareholders; (iv)
                          crowding out of private investment; (v) opportunity cost of public funds
                          (healthcare, education, climate).
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) size of resilience externality · plausibly very
                          large given the $1tr global market; (ii) subsidy design (conditionality,
                          clawbacks, export-control compliance); (iii) international coordination to
                          avoid pure subsidy race; (iv) counterfactual · without the Act, US
                          leading-edge share would likely have fallen to near zero.
                          • Judgement: on narrow productive-efficiency grounds the Act is distortive,
                          but given the scale of the positive externality and other jurisdictions'
                          behaviour, it is plausibly welfare-improving · conditional on design.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–5          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented but with limited attempt to address
                               the question. Comparisons and judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      6–9          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, clear chains of reasoning, with well-developed arguments. An
                               awareness of the significance of competing arguments is present although this may
                               lack balance.

     Level 4      10–12        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen, logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full
                               understanding of the question. Arguments are developed and evaluated. A full and
                               balanced awareness of the validity and significance of competing arguments.

 Demonstrating application (AO2) in responses
 Where questions specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus

9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 9

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      Question       Using Extracts B, C and D and your own knowledge, evaluate whether the                             Mark
      Number         ongoing 'technology decoupling' between the United States and China is
                     likely to accelerate or hinder global economic growth over the next decade.

                     Indicative content

      1(d)            Knowledge/understanding 4, Application 4, Analysis 6, Evaluation 6                                (20)

                          • Knowledge: comparative advantage and gains from trade; scale
                          economies and learning; specialisation vs duplication; R&D; spillovers;
                          investment and capital deepening; global TFP growth; geopolitical risk
                          premium; fiscal crowding-out.
                          • Application: Extract B · successive export controls (2022, 2023, 2024);
                          Extract C · Taiwan concentration and cyclicality (DRAM prices −60%);
                          Extract D · Nvidia revenue $27bn → $125bn+; 'compliant' variants
                          A800/H20 subsequently restricted. Figure 1 · China's share of production
                          rose from 0% (1990) to 19% (2024). Figure 2 · China announced $142bn
                          capex vs US $223bn. Figure 3 · Nvidia R&D; $5.3bn (FY22) → $12bn
                          (FY25).
                          • Macro diagram: AD/AS showing higher investment shifting SRAS/LRAS
                          right in advanced economies vs a negative supply-side shock from
                          fragmentation; or PPF showing loss of world output from duplication.
                          • Growth-accelerating channels: (i) massive investment stimulus · trillions
                          in capital formation globally; multiplier and accelerator effects; (ii) competing
                          R&D; effort plausibly accelerates innovation (space-race logic); (iii)
                          reshoring creates middle-income advanced-economy manufacturing jobs;
                          (iv) reduces catastrophic tail risk of supply-chain disruption · lower risk
                          premia on investment; (v) government-led investment can correct market
                          under-provision of resilience.
                          • Growth-hindering channels: (i) loss of efficiency from abandoning
                          comparative advantage; IMF estimates fragmentation cost at 0.2–7% of
                          global GDP; (ii) reduced diffusion of technology to China slows productivity
                          in the world's second-largest economy; (iii) escalating export-control
                          uncertainty deters private investment; (iv) compliant-variant cycle (A800 →
                          H20 → further limits) imposes redesign costs; (v) fragmented R&D; ·
                          duplication rather than complementary specialisation; (vi)
                          commodity/supply-chain cost-push inflation; (vii) crowding out of other
                          public investment.
                          • Synoptic micro + macro: micro · some firms strengthened (TSMC,
                          Samsung, Nvidia in Western markets; SMIC and Huawei in China), others
                          weakened (Nvidia in China, ASML's China revenue). Macro · net effect
                          depends on whether additional investment (demand-side) outweighs
                          efficiency loss (supply-side); medium-run investment boom dominates,
                          long-run efficiency loss accumulates unless R&D; effort genuinely
                          accelerates innovation.
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) completeness of decoupling · partial
                          decoupling at the frontier with continued cooperation on mature nodes is
                          more benign than full bifurcation; (ii) response of non-aligned economies
                          (India, Vietnam, Mexico); (iii) AI demand (Extract D) · may drive
                          productivity gains offsetting fragmentation losses; (iv) crisis risk · a Taiwan
                          contingency dominates all other factors.
                          • A nuanced judgement · e.g. 'growth-positive in short run via investment
                          but growth-negative in long run unless offset by AI productivity; balance
                          depends on policy design and non-aligned-economy behaviour' · required
                          for Level 4.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
     Level 1      1–4          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted, but fail to connect
                               causes and consequences. Limited attempt to address the question.

     Level 2      5–9          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented, but connections between causes
                               and consequences are incomplete. Limited attempt to address the question.
                               Comparisons, judgements or conclusions may be attempted, but are unsupported or
                               generic.

     Level 3      10–15        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, developed chains of reasoning, showing understanding of
                               connections between causes and consequences. Arguments are well developed and
                               competing arguments are present although this may lack balance. A conclusion may
                               be attempted but may not show awareness of the significance of competing
                               arguments.

     Level 4      16–20        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen and fully integrated to support the argument, well
                               developed and logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full understanding of
                               the question. Arguments are fully developed and evaluated. A full awareness of the
                               validity and significance of competing arguments, leading to nuanced and balanced
                               comparisons, judgements or conclusions.

 Where questions specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:

    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.
 Where questions do not specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must select relevant examples from their own knowledge, these must be directly connected to
    the context/issues set out in the question, and
    • may directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in any relevant stimulus.


9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 11

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
 Section B · Question 2
 Case-study context: generative AI · foundation models, Nvidia, labour market and regulation.

      Question       Using Figure 3 (Extract D) and Figure 4, calculate the percentage-point                            Mark
      Number         change in Nvidia's gross margin between FY23 and FY24, and Nvidia's
                     estimated share of 2024 AI chip sales. Discuss what these data suggest
                     about Nvidia's degree of market power.

                     Indicative content

      2(a)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 2, Evaluation 2                              (8)

                          • Gross margin change: 72.7 − 56.9 = +15.8 percentage points. Accept also
                          the relative change (72.7/56.9 − 1) ≈ +27.8% if clearly explained.
                          • Nvidia's share of 2024 AI chip sales: approximate annualised Nvidia AI
                          chip revenue ≈ $100bn (midpoint of FY24 $60.9bn and FY25 est. $125bn).
                          Total market $145bn (Figure 4). Share ≈ 100/145 ≈ 69%. Accept a range
                          60–85% with working.
                          • Market-power indicators: gross margin rose from 56.9% to 72.7% · an
                          extraordinary expansion consistent with pricing power (P >> MC).
                          Combined with ~69% market share, this is strong evidence of substantial
                          market power.
                          • Demand is highly inelastic · no close substitutes at the frontier, CUDA
                          software lock-in raises switching costs. Supply is constrained (TSMC
                          advanced nodes).
                          • Operating profit rose from $4.2bn (FY23) to $32bn (FY24); market cap
                          from $370bn to $1.22tr · financial market expects rent capture to continue.
                          • Evaluation: durability depends on (i) DeepSeek (Extract H) showed
                          lower-cost inference is possible; (ii) custom silicon from hyperscalers
                          (Google TPUs, Amazon Trainium) competes on inference; (iii) AMD MI300
                          and Intel Gaudi compete directly; (iv) CUDA lock-in may erode as open
                          alternatives mature; (v) antitrust attention.
                          • Judgement: Nvidia exhibits substantial market power in the short run;
                          long-run sustainability much less certain.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–5          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are developed. Judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      6–8          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen. Arguments are developed, using logical, coherent
                               chains of reasoning. A balanced awareness of competing arguments.

 Demonstrating application (AO2) in responses
 Where questions specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus
    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.
 Where questions do not specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must select relevant examples from their own knowledge, these must be directly connected to
    the context/issues set out in the question, and
    • may directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in any relevant stimulus.

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      Question       With reference to Extract F, assess the likely macroeconomic consequences                          Mark
      Number         of the rise in AI-related data-centre electricity demand for the economies that
                     host major data centres.

                     Indicative content

      2(b)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 3, Evaluation 3                              (10)

                          • Knowledge: aggregate demand (investment, government spending),
                          aggregate supply shifts, capacity utilisation, inflation, crowding out, regional
                          multiplier, externalities (carbon), public goods (grid infrastructure).
                          • Extract F: global data-centre electricity could double by 2026;
                          hyperscalers have signed 30 GW of low-carbon contracts, including nuclear
                          restart (Three Mile Island). Figure 4 · hyperscaler capex $128bn →
                          $235bn over three years.
                          • Positive macro effects: (i) investment boom · $235bn hyperscaler capex
                          directly raises AD; (ii) regional multiplier · DC construction employs
                          thousands locally; (iii) anchor demand for clean power reduces financing
                          costs for decarbonisation; (iv) productivity spillovers from cloud computing
                          across the wider economy.
                          • Negative macro effects: (i) pressure on electricity prices · doubling of DC
                          demand can crowd out other users (Ireland has imposed a moratorium on
                          new DCs in Dublin); (ii) cost-push inflationary pressure via higher business
                          electricity prices; (iii) local externalities · water use, grid strain; (iv)
                          concentration in a few regions amplifies local pressures; (v) if supply
                          expansion relies on new nuclear (5–10+ yr lead time), short-run demand
                          must be met by gas, undermining climate targets.
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) pace of supply expansion vs demand; (ii)
                          location · economies with abundant renewable potential benefit more than
                          constrained grids; (iii) productivity payoff · if Goldman's 1.5pp TFP gain
                          materialises, the cost is trivially small relative to the benefit; (iv) regulatory
                          response (siting policies, capacity payments).
                          • Judgement: economies with surplus clean power and investment-friendly
                          regulation are likely net beneficiaries; those with constrained grids face
                          macro-inflationary pressure in the short run.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–4          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented but with limited attempt to address
                               the question. Comparisons and judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      5–7          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, clear chains of reasoning, with well-developed arguments. An
                               awareness of the significance of competing arguments is present although this may
                               lack balance.

     Level 4      8–10         Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen, logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full
                               understanding of the question. Arguments are developed and evaluated. A full and
                               balanced awareness of the validity and significance of competing arguments.

    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus

      Question       With reference to Extract G, discuss the likely impact of widespread                               Mark
      Number         adoption of generative AI on the labour market and income distribution in
                     advanced economies.

                     Indicative content

      2(c)              Knowledge/understanding 2, Application 2, Analysis 4, Evaluation 4                              (12)

                          • Technology-driven labour effects: substitution (jobs replaced),
                          complementarity (productivity enhanced), creation (new tasks). Skill-biased
                          vs routine-biased technical change. Polarisation hypothesis. Factor shares
                          (capital vs labour).
                          • Extract G: Goldman 1.5pp annual productivity; MIT/Princeton task-level
                          gains of 14–56%; IMF · 40% of global jobs exposed, 60% in advanced
                          economies, higher exposure for women and higher earners.
                          • Labour-market effects: (i) substitution in cognitive routine tasks (customer
                          service, basic coding, legal review, copywriting); (ii) complementarity ·
                          AI-augmented workers become more productive (a programmer with
                          Copilot produces more per hour); (iii) creation of new roles (prompt
                          engineering, AI safety, data curation, AI-ethics); (iv) frictional/structural
                          unemployment during transition; (v) value of generic cognitive skill falls;
                          value of directing AI rises.
                          • Income-distribution effects: (i) unlike previous waves, generative AI
                          exposes higher-earning white-collar work · top-decile labour share may
                          fall, but capital share rises · net effect on inequality ambiguous; (ii) gender
                          dimension · IMF notes female-dominated roles have higher exposure; (iii)
                          between-country inequality · advanced economies more exposed; (iv)
                          factor shares · returns to AI capital (Nvidia gross margin 72%+) may shift
                          factor income shares.
                          • Evaluation: depends on (i) pace of capability growth · fast diffusion
                          amplifies displacement; (ii) complementarity vs substitution balance; (iii)
                          policy response · reskilling investment, social safety net, capital taxation;
                          (iv) competition in AI markets · more competitive supply passes more
                          surplus to labour/consumers; (v) new-job creation rate · historically
                          technology creates more jobs than it destroys, but transitions are painful.
                          • Judgement: the short-run vs long-run asymmetry, distributional
                          complexity, and policy-dependence are critical; a nuanced answer is
                          rewarded at Level 4.

     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–2          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted. Limited attempt to
                               address the question.

     Level 2      3–5          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented but with limited attempt to address
                               the question. Comparisons and judgements may be attempted.

     Level 3      6–9          Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, clear chains of reasoning, with well-developed arguments. An
                               awareness of the significance of competing arguments is present although this may
                               lack balance.

     Level 4      10–12        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen, logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full
                               understanding of the question. Arguments are developed and evaluated. A full and
                               balanced awareness of the validity and significance of competing arguments.

 Demonstrating application (AO2) in responses

 Where questions specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus
    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.
 Where questions do not specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must select relevant examples from their own knowledge, these must be directly connected to
    the context/issues set out in the question, and
    • may directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in any relevant stimulus.


9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                       Page 16

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      Question       Evaluate the appropriate role for governments in regulating the market for                         Mark
      Number         frontier AI foundation models, using evidence from Extracts E to H. Your
                     answer should consider both microeconomic efficiency and wider
                     macroeconomic consequences.

                     Indicative content


9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                              Page 17

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
      2(d)            Knowledge/understanding 4, Application 4, Analysis 6, Evaluation 6                                (20)

                        • Knowledge: market failure · externalities (mis/disinformation, bias,
                        safety), public goods (AI safety research), concentration/monopoly,
                        asymmetric information. Government failure · regulatory capture,
                        compliance cost, innovation chill, extraterritorial spillovers. Dynamic vs
                        static efficiency; precautionary principle.
                        • Application: Extract E · winner-take-all stack with hyperscaler–AI-firm
                        partnerships; Extract F · training costs ($100m → potentially $10bn),
                        energy demand; Extract G · EU AI Act with 7% turnover fines, US
                        executive orders; Extract H · Microsoft–OpenAI $13bn,
                        Amazon–Google–Anthropic up to $8bn; DeepSeek open-weight release
                        ($600bn market-cap wipe); antitrust investigations.
                        • Micro case FOR regulation: (i) externalities · misinformation,
                        discriminatory outputs, cyber-offensive use, concentration in critical
                        infrastructure; (ii) asymmetric information · users cannot evaluate model
                        safety, so disclosure rules help; (iii) concentration · hyperscaler
                        partnerships may foreclose competing developers from cloud distribution;
                        (iv) public-goods case for government support of AI safety research; (v)
                        consumer protection against misleading AI content.
                        • Micro case AGAINST: (i) regulatory cost disadvantages smaller
                        developers (Mistral, open-source) relative to hyperscaler-backed
                        incumbents · entrenching concentration; (ii) prescriptive regulation stifles
                        dynamic efficiency; (iii) hard to specify regulation for rapidly evolving
                        technology; (iv) DeepSeek episode shows frontier capability may emerge at
                        far lower cost than assumed · reason to hesitate before regulating on
                        winner-take-all assumptions; (v) regulatory capture · major labs may
                        shape rules to raise rivals' costs.
                        • Macro case FOR: (i) mitigates labour-market disruption via impact
                        assessments / phased deployment; (ii) redistributive measures (data-worker
                        rights, training levies) moderate inequality growth; (iii) supports financial
                        stability by constraining AI use in high-risk applications; (iv) reduces
                        geopolitical instability (bioweapon assistance, disinformation).
                        • Macro case AGAINST: (i) slower adoption reduces productivity dividend
                        · if Goldman's 1.5pp TFP estimate is correct, a one-year delay costs
                        trillions of dollars of global output; (ii) regulatory fragmentation · EU AI Act,
                        US executive orders, China registration · shrinks global addressable
                        market; (iii) innovation flight to lighter-touch jurisdictions; (iv) hyperscaler
                        capex ($235bn in 2024) could be deterred if regulatory uncertainty raises
                        required returns.
                        • Synoptic integration: micro and macro considerations cut in different
                        directions for different instruments. Antitrust (e.g. blocking Microsoft's equity
                        link to OpenAI) addresses micro concentration, limited macro cost.
                        Safety/disclosure obligations (EU AI Act) address micro externalities with
                        macro productivity cost only if poorly calibrated. Ex-ante licensing of frontier
                        training imposes large macro costs; ex-post liability imposes lower macro
                        cost but may under-deter.
                        • Evaluation: depends on (i) empirical magnitude of AI harms · uncertain,
                        arguing for an adaptive framework; (ii) international coordination vs
                        fragmentation; (iii) specific instrument · competition enforcement vs safety
                        disclosure vs licensing; (iv) speed of adaptation; (v) allocation of cost
                        (developers vs deployers); (vi) treatment of open-weight models.
                        • A nuanced judgement · e.g. 'intervention appropriate for clearly-identified
                        externalities (competition, safety disclosure, high-risk uses) but should
                        avoid prescriptive ex-ante licensing, preserving the dynamic efficiency on
                        which the macroeconomic productivity dividend depends' · required for
                        Level 4.


9EB0/03 · Predicted MS (Advanced)                                                                                              Page 18

                   Predicted practice paper · produced for revision only; not an official Pearson / Edexcel document.
     Level        Mark         Descriptor

                      0        A completely inaccurate response.

     Level 1      1–4          Isolated elements of knowledge and understanding, using little or no relevant
                               evidence. Arguments and chains of reasoning may be attempted, but fail to connect
                               causes and consequences. Limited attempt to address the question.

     Level 2      5–9          Elements of knowledge and understanding, using limited relevant evidence.
                               Arguments and chains of reasoning are presented, but connections between causes
                               and consequences are incomplete. Limited attempt to address the question.
                               Comparisons, judgements or conclusions may be attempted, but are unsupported or
                               generic.

     Level 3      10–15        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported by use of relevant evidence to
                               support the argument, developed chains of reasoning, showing understanding of
                               connections between causes and consequences. Arguments are well developed and
                               competing arguments are present although this may lack balance. A conclusion may
                               be attempted but may not show awareness of the significance of competing
                               arguments.

     Level 4      16–20        Accurate knowledge and understanding, supported throughout by use of relevant
                               evidence which is well chosen and fully integrated to support the argument, well
                               developed and logical, coherent chains of reasoning, showing full understanding of
                               the question. Arguments are fully developed and evaluated. A full awareness of the
                               validity and significance of competing arguments, leading to nuanced and balanced
                               comparisons, judgements or conclusions.

 Demonstrating application (AO2) in responses
 Where questions specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in the stimulus
    • in addition, may select examples from their own knowledge but these must be relevant and
    directly connected to the context/issues set out in the stimulus.
 Where questions do not specifically stipulate the use of data or information provided in a stimulus, students:
    • must select relevant examples from their own knowledge, these must be directly connected to
    the context/issues set out in the question, and
    • may directly reference, interpret or analyse the information provided in any relevant stimulus.
